title: The Gauntlet: Schefter Ranks Every Team’s Road Ahead
summary: Claude Schefter’s new weekly column ranks every franchise’s remaining schedule from cupcake run to buzz saw — with a full dashboard, a week-by-week heat map, and receipts for whose record is lying.
description: Your record says 5-2. Congratulations. Now answer the uncomfortable question: 5-2 against WHOM? Starting this season, Claude Schefter answers it for you every Wednesday morning with The Gauntlet — his new schedule-strength column that rates every team’s remaining opponents on a 0-100 difficulty scale and ranks the league from “walking into a wood chipper” down to “booked a cruise through the soft part of the schedule.” Both leagues get their own edition, and yes, he built the model himself, and yes, he trusts it more than he trusts you.

The column is just the headline act. Behind it sits a full Schedule Strength dashboard: “The Run-In” ranks who has the hardest road left (with week-over-week trend arrows, because your schedule can get scarier while you sleep), “What They’ve Been Through” scores the slate each team has already survived, and the Schedule Luck callouts name names — the 6-1 team that’s been feasting on cupcakes, and the 1-6 team that’s secretly been playing murderers’ row. Scroll down for the week-by-week heat map: every franchise, every remaining week, color-coded green to red, each cell one click from your future opponent’s franchise page. Trap Weeks flags the Sunday the whole league hits a wall at once.

Every Wednesday the numbers recompute from settled stats, Schefter files his column to the Schefter Report, and one perfectly-calibrated teaser lands in the GroupMe — just enough to start an argument by lunch. Find Schedule Strength in site search, or wait for Wednesday and let the man tell you himself. Your record has an asterisk now. He put it there.
imageAlt: The Gauntlet schedule-strength dashboard showing the Week 9 run-in rankings with difficulty bars for every franchise

title: The Trade Block Comes to the AFL Trade Builder
summary: Players flagged on the trade block now show up right inside the AFL Trade Builder — see who is shopping, then start the trade in one click.
description: The AFL Trade Builder knew your roster, knew their roster, and knew exactly how lopsided your offer was. What it didn't know was who actually wanted to make a deal — you were cold-calling every roster in the conference like a telemarketer with a draft-pick script. That changes today: every player an owner has flagged on the trade block now headlines the Trade Builder in a new On the Trade Block panel, grouped by team, ranked by ADP.

See a player you like? Click him. If he plays for a conference rival, the builder loads that team and drops him straight into the 'They give' column — you're one counter-offer away from a done deal. Your own flagged players get the same treatment on your side of the ledger, and the little 🏷️ tag follows flagged players into the roster pickers and the team dropdown, so availability is visible everywhere you build. Teams from the other conference still show up on the block, but they're politely grayed out — cross-conference trades remain illegal, no matter how nicely you ask.

And yes, the block is now properly attributed: with both conferences drafting from the same NFL player pool, the old data couldn't tell WHICH team had flagged a player. Now it can. Flag a player from the Rosters page (the ⋯ menu next to any player) and the whole league knows you're open for business — the right you, on the right team.
imageAlt: AFL Trade Builder showing the On the Trade Block panel with flagged players grouped by team

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Jul 6-10)
summary: 21 bug fixes and style improvements across free agents, rosters, design system & theming, other, and more.
description: This week's 21 bug fixes and polish improvements touched the following areas of the site:

Free Agents — Player avatars in light mode got a lot less gloomy. The little circular headshots on the Free Agents list (and everywhere else the player lockup shows up) used to sit on a dark team-color gradient no matter the theme — heavy and hard to read against a bright white page. Light mode now uses a soft gray chip with a thin team-color ring instead; dark mode keeps its original moody gradient look untouched. Team defenses finally have a face in the free-agent pool. Tap a D/ST and, instead of a lonely logo staring back, the player card now leads with the unit's headliner — the Raiders' Maxx Crosby, say — his headshot on the team-color gradient and his name in the byline. It's the same trick the Free Agents spotlight already pulls up top, now brought into the detail card so a defense reads like the roster of real humans it actually is. The Free Agents hero stopped reaching for the shoebox photo. When the spotlighted player had no ESPN headshot, the panel used to fall back to a tiny, grainy MyFantasyLeague mugshot (or a gray "no photo" placeholder) — a blurry face is worse than none up in the hero. Now it simply hides the cutout and lets the team logo carry the panel, so the spotlight only ever shows a crisp headshot or a clean crest. Both The League and AFL boards get the fix.

Rosters — Fixed a cropped team logo on the Rosters page's Cap Hits / Dead Money table. The "[Year] Dead Money" total row shows a franchise crest instead of a player photo, but it was getting squeezed and cropped by the same circular-cutout treatment built for headshots — top of the logo clipped off. It now renders full and uncropped, just like the DEF logo chips elsewhere on the site. The "Step 1 of…" tracker at the top of the contract declaration flow stopped mumbling in the dark. That little progress label had been rendered in a dim gray that all but disappeared against the modal's dark backdrop, leaving you squinting to tell which step you were on. Bumped it to a brighter, theme-aware tone that reads cleanly in both light and dark mode — same understated label, now actually legible.

Design System & Theming — The Pacific Pigskins and Vitside Mafia crests finally got dark-mode variants. Both teams' logos used to stay stuck in their light-theme colors when you had dark mode on, standing out (in a bad way) against every other team's icon smoothly swapping over — now they blend in like everyone else, on both TheLeague and AFL sites. Team logos now dress for the dark. Marks with black outlines — the Raiders shield, the Steelers hypocycloids, the Jets, North Carolina's navy interlock — used to all but vanish against a dark background, a smudge where a logo should be. In dark mode every NFL logo across the site (matchup heroes, the composite banners, roster player rows) now swaps to its dark-optimized cut, outline flipped to white so the crest actually reads, and the college logos in the Free Agents and roster player tables get the same treatment. Light mode is untouched, and the switch follows your theme toggle in real time. The Schefter Ops dashboard finally turned the lights off. In dark mode the commissioner's rumor-mill control room had been stranded in daylight — every card stayed stark white while the page around it went dark, and the text painted on top all but vanished into the glare. Wired the whole page into the real dark theme so cards, borders, code chips and status text all dim down properly. Commish-only, so nobody else ever saw the flashbang. The "Back to MFL" strip up top got a smoother fade in dark mode. That deep-blue glow band was cutting off to fully transparent partway up, leaving a visible seam where it met the near-black page. Tuned the gradient to fade out at a soft trailing tint instead of hard zero, so the strip blends into the page instead of stopping short.

Other — Live Scoring no longer sits empty in the offseason. With the NFL season over, MyFantasyLeague switches its live feed off — so out of season, tapping Live Scoring in the nav dropped you on a barren "Scores will appear here when games begin" screen. Now, whenever the feed's dark, the page plays back the last completed regular season's final week as if it were unfolding live: real starting lineups built from the actual box score, some matchups final and others mid-play with live win-probability bars, game clocks, and projected finals that land on the real results — all flagged with a "Sample data" badge so nobody mistakes it for a live game. The instant the season kicks off and the feed lights back up, it flips to real-time scores on its own — no toggle, no special link. Live Scoring's matchup detail finally shows both rosters on a phone. Tap into a matchup and scroll to the player-by-player breakdown: your lineup sat on the left, the numbers ran across the middle, and the opponent's entire starting nine spilled clean off the right edge — you couldn't see the team you were playing without a sideways swipe. Rebuilt the row for small screens so both sides fit at once: your player on the left, theirs on the right, each tagged with its own color-coded position chip and score, real headshots on both. Long names now tuck to an ellipsis instead of bulldozing the score beside them, and nothing runs past the screen. The desktop layout — full names, team and game clock, projections — is untouched. The Live Scoring scoreboard stopped running out of bounds on phones. Tap into a matchup on your phone and the big score-header — two teams, their crests, and those jumbo live totals — had been spilling off the right edge, shoving the home team's score clean off-screen behind a phantom horizontal scroll. Reworked it so each side stacks tidily (crest and name up top, the giant number underneath) and the whole card now fits the screen with everyone visible. While we were in there, the "← All matchups" back button got a little breathing room above the scorecard instead of sitting right on top of it. Claude Schefter is back on the beat. His scanner — the one that breaks transaction news and fires Roger's event reminders — had quietly flatlined: a duplicate line snuck into its scheduler config, which is the kind of typo the robots refuse to forgive, so the whole job got rejected before it ever woke up. Every run since had failed silently. Cleared the duplicate and the newsdesk is live again. Live Scoring stopped hiding the fine print after dark. When a player was booming — matching or beating his projection — his cell lights up with a green wash, but in dark mode the little "proj" number tucked underneath stayed a dim gray that all but dissolved into that glow, so the one line telling you what he was supposed to score went unreadable right when it mattered. Tinted it a legible mint that sits cleanly on the green in dark mode (and a deep green in light), so the projection reads at a glance whether or not the player's on a heater.

Homepage — What's New finally moved into the AFL homepage. The League has had that little row of recent-update cards on its front page for a while; now AFL owners get the same feed on afl-fantasy.com, browser-frame screenshots and all, pointed at the right domain. While we were in there the whole card treatment got a glow-up on both sites: the category accent colors — the little stripe and pill on each card — were a muddy, muted set that barely registered, so they got brightened to punchier greens, blues, violets and reds, and they now light up even bolder in dark mode instead of sulking. The cards themselves stopped floating shapeless against the page and picked up the same proper border and drop-shadow depth every other card on the site already wears, in both light and dark. The "Completed auctions report" link on the homepage auctions card now stays in one piece. It had been breaking mid-phrase — "Completed" clinging to the end of one line while "auctions report" dropped to the next — so the whole link now wraps to a fresh line together when it runs out of room. The homepage and season heroes stopped whispering in daylight. In light mode the whole family — the What's New announcement, the weekly recap, the waiver and weekend-preview articles, the auction and cut-watch panels, the champion-crowned card, the AFL event hero and more — had faded to pale, near-white washes that all but vanished against the page, hoarding every bit of drama for dark mode. Now each wears a bold saturated card in every theme, keyed to its own color: deep league-blue for most, warm amber for the auction, alarm-red for breaking news and roster cuts, trophy-gold for a champion crowned. White headlines, on-brand glow, and the featured player bigger than ever — the daylight version finally hits as hard as the night one. The player on the Cut Watch hero got a growth spurt. The bubble-player cutout — the face staring back at you while the roster-deadline countdown ticks — was sitting a little small in the corner, so we sized him up to fill the frame the way he was always meant to. More presence, same panic. Mobile is untouched. The Cut Watch bubble player scooted over to claim the wall. He'd been floating a couple dozen pixels shy of the hero's right edge, leaving an awkward sliver of empty gradient between his shoulder and the frame. Now he's pinned flush to the edge where he belongs — same panic-inducing stare down the roster-deadline countdown, just anchored properly instead of drifting. Mobile is untouched.

site — Wander off the map and you no longer hit a broken-looking dead end. The old 404 page had two lines of text hard-coded to near-black, so in dark mode "Page Not Found" all but disappeared into the void. The whole page got rebuilt as a Claude Schefter breaking-news alert instead: a red BREAKING banner, Schefter's mugshot on the byline, a giant 404, the headline "Sources say this page doesn't exist," and a LIVE ticker scrolling league-flavored bulletins along the bottom ("Developing: URL reportedly entered the transfer portal"). Reads perfectly in both light and dark now, and instead of making you pick a league, a single button — labeled "Back to AFL" on afl-fantasy.com and "Back to The League" on theleague.us — drops you on the homepage of whichever site you wandered off of.

Live Scoring — Live Scoring's win-probability bars finally show their true colors after dark. A bunch of teams carry near-black or deep-navy brand colors — Music City, the Magicians, Cowboy Up — and in dark mode those bars had been vanishing into the card or getting nudged to a muddy compromise, so you couldn't tell whose slice was whose. Every franchise now has a hand-picked dark-mode primary and secondary: Music City lights up in Titans sky-blue, the Magicians in bright Dark-Magician purple, and the rest in vivid, on-brand hues that read cleanly against the dark scoreboard. Light mode is untouched.

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Jul 6-14)
summary: 16 bug fixes and style improvements across keeper-planner, rosters, design system & theming, homepage, and more.
description: This week's 16 bug fixes and polish improvements touched the following areas of the site:

keeper-planner — Finalizing keepers no longer gaslights you into thinking it failed. The cuts were landing on MFL just fine, but the page reloaded from a roster snapshot that syncs about once an hour — so your just-cut players were still staring back at you, and re-running finalize spat out a wall of scary (and wrong) errors like "You can only cut players from your own roster." Three fixes: the AFL rosters page now reads live MFL data for the current year (same 2-minute freshness TheLeague already enjoys), the finalize screen confirms your keepers are locked in with a button that shows your actual new roster, and a re-run recognizes already-cut players as done instead of calling them failures.

Rosters — AFL player avatars sized up to match TheLeague. Rosters, the NFL/College analytics grids, the trade builder, and the keeper planner were all quietly running smaller headshot circles than TheLeague's equivalents — now they're the same 40px size across both sites. The AFL Keeper Planner finally read the room. Flip on dark mode and the drag-the-line tool had stayed stubbornly, blindingly white — every keeper card, the above/below-the-line zones, the status bar and the finalize modal glowing like a lightbox on the navy page. Now the whole planner dresses in the AFL night palette: navy cards, slate borders, a green wash on the keepers you're protecting and red on the cuts. While we were in there, the Roster/Analytics/Planner toggle stopped rendering its active tab as a stark white chip, and the roster table's "Opp #" matchup badges swapped their bright pastel pills for the same muted greens and reds every other page uses after dark.

The cards themselves stopped floating shapeless against the page and picked up the same proper border and drop-shadow depth every other card on the site already wears, in both light and dark. The homepage and season heroes stopped whispering in daylight. Now each wears a bold saturated card in every theme, keyed to its own color: deep league-blue for most, warm amber for the auction, alarm-red for breaking news and roster cuts, trophy-gold for a champion crowned. site — Wander off the map and you no longer hit a broken-looking dead end. The old 404 page had two lines of text hard-coded to near-black, so in dark mode "Page Not Found" all but disappeared into the void. The whole page got rebuilt as a Claude Schefter breaking-news alert instead: a red BREAKING banner, Schefter's mugshot on the byline, a giant 404, the headline "Sources say this page doesn't exist," and a LIVE ticker scrolling league-flavored bulletins along the bottom ("Developing: URL reportedly entered the transfer portal"). Reads perfectly in both light and dark now, and instead of making you pick a league, a single button — labeled "Back to AFL" on afl-fantasy.com and "Back to The League" on theleague.us — drops you on the homepage of whichever site you wandered off of.

Standings — The Premier League standings card on the AFL homepage stopped crowding its own headline on phones. Its "Full standings" link had been wedged into the top-right corner, sharing a cramped line with the title, while the American League playoff card right above it politely dropped its "Full bracket" link onto its own row on mobile. Now the two cards match: on phones the Premier League link tucks under the heading the same way, giving the title room to breathe.

Other — Claude Schefter is back on the beat. His scanner — the one that breaks transaction news and fires Roger's event reminders — had quietly flatlined: a duplicate line snuck into its scheduler config, which is the kind of typo the robots refuse to forgive, so the whole job got rejected before it ever woke up. Every run since had failed silently. Cleared the duplicate and the newsdesk is live again.

Navigation & Routing — A handful of AFL links had been sneaking you across the border into The League. Biggest one: tapping "Set Lineup" while signed out bounced you to The League's login page instead of AFL's — now it sends you to the AFL sign-in and drops you right back on your lineup. Same treatment for the "View all" on the Schefter Report card and the Trade Builder buttons on the trade-offer popup, which had been pointing at The League's versions. And the header search icon, which had no AFL home to go to, steps aside on AFL until an all-pages search lands there.

Free Agents — The AFL Free Agents hero gave its defenses a face. Land on the DEF tab and instead of a lonely team crest, the spotlight now leads with that unit's marquee defender — his ESPN headshot front and center, the team logo faded behind as a watermark — exactly like the offensive players already get. Each defense carries a rotating cast of its top defenders, so it opens on a different face each visit and cycles through the rest while you browse. It's the same broadcast treatment The League's board has run for weeks, now brought home to AFL. The Free Agents hero stopped reaching for the shoebox photo. When the spotlighted player had no ESPN headshot, the panel used to fall back to a tiny, grainy MyFantasyLeague mugshot (or a gray "no photo" placeholder) — a blurry face is worse than none up in the hero. Now it simply hides the cutout and lets the team logo carry the panel, so the spotlight only ever shows a crisp headshot or a clean crest. Both The League and AFL boards get the fix. The AFL Free Agents page stopped sending you to the wrong stadium. Type afl-fantasy.com/players and instead of the free-agent board you'd land on a 404 wearing The League's colors — the page had been built to render ahead of time, which quietly broke the behind-the-scenes routing that maps the clean AFL web address to the right page. Rewired it to render on request, the same way its League counterpart already does, so the AFL Free Agents board now loads exactly where it should.

Calendar — The AFL calendar got its offseason clock reset to match the real thing. The homepage had the American League draft gaveling in at 9 a.m. — it's actually a 12:30 p.m. Saturday affair — while the World Championship countdown was firing a full week early, landing on Week 16 instead of the real title week (Thursday, Dec 31). The keeper-deadline lock nudged to its true 8:45 p.m., and the "new league year" rollover moved off its stale mid-February slot to June 1, when the new AFL season actually spins up on MyFantasyLeague. Every date now lines up with the league calendar to the minute.

Trade Builder — The AFL trade alert was having an identity crisis. Send an offer to Team Minty Fresh and the popup would proudly announce a trade with... Maverick? That's a TheLeague team. The modal was reading franchise numbers off the wrong league's roster sheet — AFL team #3 and TheLeague team #3 are very different people. Trade offers on the AFL side now name (and badge) the actual AFL teams involved. Bonus fix: the player headshots inside the offer had lost their styling on AFL pages and were rendering as glorious full-screen portraits. They're back to sensible thumbnail size.

title: Throwback Week: The Old Names Ride Again
summary: Every Week 4, the league's weekly pages dust off the archives. Live scoring, matchups, and submit lineup swap every team's name, logo, banner — and colors — for a legacy identity from the franchise's own history. Thirty-seven eras of recovered art, from the Sabertooths to the skull wabbit. One week a season, the scoreboard becomes a family photo album.
description: Sixteen franchises, nearly two decades of rebrands, and a commissioner who spent a weekend spelunking the Wayback Machine so none of it stays buried. Every season, for Week 4 only, the weekly pages — live scoring, the matchup scoreboard, and submit lineup — roll every team back to an old identity. Old name, old logo, old banner, and yes, old colors: the matchup washes and win-probability bars tint in each era's own palette, sampled from the era's own art. Thirty-seven eras made the cut, every one wearing genuine recovered artwork — Degenerates green, Sabertooths orange, the Barney Fife bullseye.

Each team defaults to a commissioner-picked former self — Heavy Chevy gives way to the Drunk Indians, the Computer Jocks take the floor as the Witch City Warlocks, and Maverick puckers up as the 2012 'smooch' Poker in the Rear. Rather choose your own ghost? Throwback Settings lets every owner browse their franchise's eligible eras — same-name eras get their own nicknames ('The razorback', 'The skull', 'Red graffiti era') so you always know which vintage you're buying. Hit Preview on any era to see it live on the scoreboard before committing, then save it right from the preview when it looks right. Your pick sticks for every future Throwback Week until you change it.

The rest of the site keeps its head: standings, rosters, and the trade builder stay on current identities, so nobody's wondering who the Sabertooths are in the middle of a trade negotiation. (Only one team can be the Sabertooths anyway — we checked. It's the Geeks.) Then Week 5 arrives, everyone snaps back to the present, and the archives go back to sleep for another year. See you next September, Warlocks.
imageAlt: The live scoring scoreboard during Throwback Week, with matchup cards showing legacy franchise identities — old team names, logos, and banners from each franchise's history.

title: Set Contract Years Without Leaving the Homepage
summary: The Unsigned FA Adds card used to just point a finger at your default 1-year deals and send you off to Manage Contracts to actually do something about it. Now it does something about it right there.
description: The homepage has always been good at telling on you — 'hey, this pickup is still sitting on the default 1-year contract' — but it never let you fix it without a detour. Click through, find the player again, declare the years, hope you remembered the deadline. Small tax, paid every single time.

Tax repealed. Every eligible pickup on the card now has a Set years button that opens the same contract declaration flow the roster page uses — pick a term, hit declare, done, all without the card ever losing your place. And the card got smarter about who it shows: any player still inside the 48-hour (or 24-hour in-season) window now shows up, even after you've already declared — they just slide into a quieter Declared section with a status pill and a Change years button, in case you change your mind before the window closes.
Once the clock runs out, they age off the list either way — declared or not. The homepage was already the first thing you saw every visit; now it's also a place you can actually finish something.
imageAlt: The homepage Unsigned FA Adds card showing a player pickup with a 'Set years' button, and a contract declaration modal open with year-length options and a Declare Contract button.

title: What's New, Right on the Homepage
summary: You shouldn't have to go hunting to learn what changed. The two latest updates now ride at the bottom of the homepage's left column — a quick glance at what shipped while you were off tinkering with your lineup, no detour required.
description: For a while now the full What's New archive has lived a click away on its own page — perfect for a deep dive, a little much for a quick 'wait, what's different?' glance. So we brought the highlights home.

The two newest updates now sit at the bottom of the homepage's left column, just beneath Explore — each a tappable card with its headline, date, and a one-line pitch. 'All releases →' still takes you to the full history whenever you want the director's cut. And if a story's already headlining the hero up top, the panel quietly steps aside and shows the next one down, so you never read the same news twice on one screen.
It's a small thing that does a big thing: the stuff we ship stops being a secret you have to look up. Glance down, see what's new, get back to your roster.
imageAlt: The bottom of the homepage's left column showing a 'What's New' panel with two update cards side by side — each with a category badge, headline, date, and short summary — above an 'All releases' link.

title: Live Scoring Comes Home
summary: The Sunday scoreboard used to live on MyFantasyLeague's servers, in a gray grid of numbers with all the drama of a spreadsheet. No more. Live Scoring is now ours — every matchup, every point, projected finals and win probability, updating through the Thursday, Sunday, and Monday windows and dressed in your team's colors.
description: For years, checking who was winning meant bouncing out to the old MFL live-scoring page — a table that looked drawn in 2009. We rebuilt it from scratch, on our own turf, and gave it the broadcast treatment it always deserved.

The scoreboard floats the closest games to the top and pins your matchup up top in a card washed in both franchises' colors. Every matchup carries a live score, a projected final, and a win-probability bar that swings as your guys score — because 'you're up 12' and 'you're up 12 with their whole backfield yet to play' are very different Sundays.

Tap any matchup for the full head-to-head: starter by starter, live points over projection, each player's NFL team logo and live game state — green for on the field, amber for yet to play, a quiet gray once he's done. Beat your projection and the number lights up. The score header stays pinned while you scroll, so you never lose sight of the damage. It refreshes every 60 seconds through game windows, and yes — it was built for late nights, with a proper dark mode.
imageAlt: The Live Scoring scoreboard in dark mode: a featured 'Your Matchup' card washed from red to purple with the Pigskins and Magicians crests, live scores 64.3 and 76.9, projected finals, and a red-and-purple win-probability bar, above two smaller matchup cards.

title: AFL Free Agents, In-House
summary: A dedicated free-agent board — browse, sort, and filter every available player without leaving for MFL.
description: The Free Agents button in the header used to bounce you out to MFL's bare add/drop screen. Now it lands on a proper scouting board built right here — every available player laid out in one sortable, filterable table, with the top name of whatever you're looking at spotlighted broadcast-style up top.

Sort by projected points, dynasty ADP, or last season's total; filter by NFL team, age, height, weight, experience, and draft round; and tap the position pills to jump straight to the QBs, backs, receivers, tight ends, kickers, or team defenses on the market. Flip on "Include rostered" or "Include rookies" when you want the full pool. Click any name for the full player card.

It's a leaner cousin of the board the other league runs — no cap, contract, salary, or auction columns, because this is a keeper league that redrafts every offseason. Just the numbers that help you decide who to chase.
imageAlt: The AFL Free Agents page — a broadcast-style hero spotlighting the top available player over a faded NFL team logo, position filter pills, and a sortable table of players with age, draft, ADP, last-year and projected points columns

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Jul 2-7)
summary: 38 bug fixes and style improvements across trade builder, homepage, rosters, playoffs, and more.
description: This week's 38 bug fixes and polish improvements touched the following areas of the site:

Trade Builder — Saving a trade draft now tells you what happened. The Save Draft button flashes "Saving…" then "Saved ✓", and if the save actually fails — storage hiccup, dropped connection — a plain-English error slides in right under the button instead of the click quietly going nowhere. Same for deleting and renaming drafts from the My Trades panel. Previously every failure was swallowed silently, so a broken save looked identical to a working one and you'd just keep clicking. Drafts are still a convenience, never a blocker.

Homepage — On phones, the homepage heroes — the What's New announcement and the red Breaking News banner — no longer type their headlines directly across the featured player's face. The text now keeps to its own column on the left, and the player gets the respect he deserves: anchored to the bottom-right corner, standing nearly the full height of the card, with his left edge fading softly into the gradient instead of ending in a hard rectangle behind the shoulders. Even on the narrowest screens, the two stay out of each other's way. The trade deadline countdown hero no longer risks flashing wrong numbers when the page loads. The countdown briefly showed the server's idea of the time remaining before the browser corrected it — on deadline day the digits could visibly disagree for a beat (and in date-testing mode the clock read a surreal 3,265 hours). It now waits for the browser clock and counts down cleanly from the first tick. Claude Schefter's homepage article heroes — the waiver report and the Friday weekend preview — now put a face to the story. The hero features whichever player the article actually leans on (the biggest waiver bid, the marquee projected starter), composited over his real NFL team's colors, instead of a generic card. If a player's photo is missing, his team logo stands in rather than an empty frame. Same treatment in light and dark mode. Franchise milestone posts in the Schefter Report now say what they're announcing — a labeled badge chip (like "🎯 Career Milestone · Playoff Veteran") sits above the one-liner, and every milestone post links straight to that team's badge wall. Previously you got a cryptic quip like "Not a tourist anymore" and were left to guess what happened. The homepage hero and event cards (What's Next and the full League Calendar) now come in a proper light-mode outfit — clean white editorial cards keyed to each event's accent color — while the signature deep-navy chrome is reserved for dark mode. Same cards, correct wardrobe for the lighting.

Rosters — Player pop-ups got the hero treatment. Click any player on the rosters page and the modal now opens with a mini composite banner — his team's colors as the gradient, a ghost "QB RAVENS" wordmark behind him, and his cutout headshot bleeding off the edge, same visual language as the homepage heroes but at pop-up size. The injury pop-up goes a shade darker: the team color blends toward alert-red and the injury status itself looms behind the name. Defenses and players without a clean cutout get the gradient-and-wordmark treatment on its own, which still beats the old plain white header by a mile. The roster page's team switcher (and the team icons on Salary Analytics, the Cap Calculator, and the footer's defending-champion medallion) now show each club's current logo. Teams with a former identity — the Pigskins, Bring The Pain, Midwestside Connection, and the Dark Magicians of Chaos — were flying their retired 2007-era circle logo because the icon list was read oldest-first; it now skips the archived art and lands on the live logo. The Potential Targets card on the League Planner no longer shows up two, three, or even four times in a row for teams with a storied rebrand history — the planner was quietly rendering one copy per former team identity (Poker in the Rear and the Georgia Punishers don't need free agent advice anymore). Exactly one copy now renders for every team at every screen size.

Playoffs — The homepage playoff heroes now wear their team's actual colors. The semifinal panels, the championship stage, and the wild-card glow used to tint from a chart color picked for looking distinct on a bar graph — fine for a spreadsheet, a little random on a hero. Every franchise now has a hand-tuned brand primary (pulled from its real logo and crest), so the Pigskins panel runs deep red, Bring The Pain goes black, Midwestside glows Hawkeye gold, and the title-game field melts from one team's color through black into the other's. Same crests, same layout — just the right paint.

Calendar — Event windows and day boundaries across the site now flip on Pacific Time, not the server's clock. The production server lives in UTC, so "active now" banners lit up around 2 AM, deadlines read "Completed" by early afternoon on deadline day, and "Today" became "Tomorrow" at 5 PM. The server has been told what time zone the league actually lives in. Event countdowns on the homepage What's Next cards and the League Calendar now count real calendar days. Events with a set time (deadlines, drafts) were shown as "1 day out" all morning on the day they actually happen — and occasionally "2 days out" for tomorrow's event. Day-of events now say "Today," which is when you most need them to.

What's New — Feature announcements in the homepage hero now send you somewhere useful — the button links straight to the full article about that feature. Previously, announcements without a destination page pointed at the What's New listing (or, in one memorable case, at the very homepage you were already standing on). The Lost Archives announcement in the homepage hero now stars what it's actually about: the recovered vintage Mistakes Were Made logo button, captioned "Circa 2007 · Recovered," instead of a rookie who wasn't even born when that logo was drawn. Announcements can now bring their own artwork whenever an image is the story. The League's homepage hero and What's New page no longer pick up AFL-only announcements — an untagged AFL feature (the franchise trophy room) had been headlining The League's homepage. League tagging is now mandatory and fails closed, so cross-league mix-ups can't happen again.

Free Agents — The Free Agents page hero now spotlights whoever sits atop your current view — a broadcast-style panel with the player's ESPN headshot over their NFL team logo, updating live as you switch position tabs or re-sort (flip to QB and the panel becomes the top quarterback). The panel is styled for each theme — a light backdrop in light mode, a dark broadcast panel in dark mode — with the player's name on a label that stays readable over any jersey or logo, and the position filter pills now have a frosted-glass finish. The old rotating stock photos are gone, and the "See projected FAs" link is now a proper button you can actually see against the panel. The Free Agents hero spotlight now puts a face on team defenses. Land on the DEF tab and instead of a lonely team crest, the panel shows that unit's marquee defenders — Maxx Crosby for the Raiders, T.J. Watt for the Steelers, Micah Parsons for Green Bay — their ESPN headshot front and center with the team logo faded behind as a watermark, exactly like the offensive-player treatment. Each defense carries a rotating cast of its top six defenders — Pro Bowlers and All-Pros are pinned to the front, with the rest ranked by real production so pass-rushers and ballhawks lead — and the whole roster refreshes itself weekly from live NFL rosters, so when a star changes teams his new crest is waiting for him. Teams without a clear face fall back to the clean logo-only look.

Navigation & Routing — On phones, the Owner Activity page no longer stretches wider than the screen — the transaction and last-seen tables now scroll within their own lane, and the navigation drawer no longer peeks out along the right edge of the page. The 2026 Rookie Rankings card in the page directory and homepage quick links has its icon back — it had been pointing at an icon that doesn't exist and rendering blank. Every icon in the directory is now checked against the real icon set automatically, so a missing icon can't quietly ship again. The hamburger menu button in the top bar dropped its glossy background plate — no more lit-up pill sitting behind it in dark mode, and no more highlight that stuck around after a tap on mobile. Three clean lines, nothing else. The same cleanup went out to the forum's embedded nav, so the button looks identical everywhere you meet it.

Other — The Former Identities strip on the Franchise History page no longer sends you on links to nowhere. Identities that belonged to a prior owner of a franchise slot (Mistakes Were Made, Devil Dogs, and friends) used to link to an era section that doesn't exist on the current owner's page — they now take you to that identity's vintage art in the Asset Library instead, and identities from a current owner's tenure still jump straight to their era. The delete-confirmation dialog in the Mock Draft lobby now gets the site's signature frosted-glass backdrop — the page behind it softly blurs instead of just dimming, matching every other modal on the site. Owner Activity page content now lines up flush with the header and logo above it — previously the page was noticeably narrower and off-center from the nav bar, at every screen size from phone to desktop. The matchup data debug page no longer crashes with a server error during the off-season — team projection math now handles MFL's empty off-season projections feed gracefully (and any page using team projections is protected from the same quirk). The Asset Library's title banner is readable again — it had been rendering white text on a near-white background, making "The League Asset Library" heading effectively invisible. The banner now wears the dark slate look it was always meant to have. Historical team identities now have proper circular icons, cropped from the best part of each vintage banner (mascots and emblems over wordmarks), and the Asset Library is reorganized into Active Teams, Former Teams, Championship Art, League Logos, Conferences, and Divisions — with a card for every legacy identity showing its banner, icon, and the years (and conference) it was active. Cleaned up a handful of duplicate and mismatched icons in the Asset Library — a few AFL teams (Chatmaster, Computer Jocks, Da Dangsters) were showing an extra auto-generated icon alongside their real one, and the League's crest logo was rendering oversized in the League Logos section. Long file names in the Asset Library no longer spill out of their cards — they now truncate cleanly with an ellipsis, and every asset card has one-click copy buttons for both the file name and the full URL (clicking the URL field also selects the whole thing). The players stand taller in the matchup faceoff card. The two-star VS panel — atop the Set Lineup page and across the matchup surfaces — now sizes each player's cutout by height so he fills the panel, with the top of his head reaching the center VS badge instead of floating low in the frame. Same panel, bigger stars.

Salary — A handful of pages had been quietly haunted by the league's past lives — the Salary Analytics player tables credited players to retired identities like Under Siege and Devil Dogs instead of their current franchises, League Comparison and the draft capital table double-counted historical rebrands, and the roster page's draft cards could pull a vintage banner. Every franchise lookup now resolves to the team's current identity; the ghosts stay in the Asset Library where they belong.

Design System & Theming — The League's Asset Library now matches the AFL's organization — division badges live in their own Divisions section under league assets instead of masquerading as team cards in the alphabetical team grid.

Import Rankings — The Import Rankings pop-ups (import details and delete confirmation) now dim the page behind them with the site's signature frosted-glass blur instead of a plain dark shade — matching the site's modal standard.

Standings — Franchise history and career records were quietly undercounting years — 2007-2018 was missing week 17 entirely, several seasons between 2019-2024 were missing several weeks outright, and even recent years could drop a team's score when its game didn't fit the usual matchup format. All 2007-2025 seasons now carry every week's real scores, so highest-game and biggest-blowout records reflect what actually happened (turns out someone had a bigger week than anyone knew about). The League's division standings finally look the part. Each division table is now topped by a branded blue banner: the division's own compass badge on the left (Northwest, Southwest, Central, East each get their own mark), the division name in big condensed caps, and the defending champion's logo riding shotgun on a white medallion right beside it. The old plain-text "Division Champions: …" line is gone — the hardware speaks for itself now. Historical standings pages now show every team's real banner from that season — nine or more rows per year had broken images pointing at the league's long-dead old websites. Vintage banners for the Degenerates, Las Vegas Elite, Devil Dogs, Executioners, Sabertooths, and a dozen more were recovered from the Internet Archive and the league's old MFL records. The 2007, 2010, 2011, and 2015 season records were quietly wrong — those years had been synced from other leagues that happened to share an MFL league ID. Standings, schedules, playoff brackets, and weekly scores for those seasons now reflect The League's actual history (2015 has all 16 teams again). The 2007-2017 stretch of Dead Cap Walking's history had been split into two mismatched records — a 2017 entry wrongly showed The Art of War's artwork under the Las Vegas Elite name. The League's own records confirm 2007-2017 was continuously Las Vegas Elite; that era now shows its real banner and icon, with The Art of War's brief 2018 run as its own distinct entry. On phones, the Division / Playoff / All-Play switcher at the top of the Standings page no longer wraps its last button onto a second row. The three tabs now share the width and sit shoulder-to-shoulder on a single line, even on the narrowest screens or when your phone's text is scaled up. Both The League and the AFL standings pages get the fix.
imageAlt: The homepage Weekend Preview hero featuring CeeDee Lamb's headshot composited over a Cowboys-blue gradient, with a 'One to Watch · WR · DAL' caption

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Jun 30-Jul 7)
summary: 33 bug fixes and style improvements across rosters, calendar, homepage, what's new, and more.
description: This week's 33 bug fixes and polish improvements touched the following areas of the site:

Rosters — Player pop-ups got the hero treatment. Click any player on the rosters page and the modal now opens with a mini composite banner — his team's colors as the gradient, a ghost "QB RAVENS" wordmark behind him, and his cutout headshot bleeding off the edge, same visual language as the homepage heroes but at pop-up size. The injury pop-up goes a shade darker: the team color blends toward alert-red and the injury status itself looms behind the name. Defenses and players without a clean cutout get the gradient-and-wordmark treatment on its own, which still beats the old plain white header by a mile. In the Keeper Planner, the "send back to cut pool" arrows on your keepers now match the polished icon-button look of the "keep" arrows below the line — they had been rendering as bare unstyled browser buttons.

Calendar — Event windows and day boundaries across the site now flip on Pacific Time, not the server's clock. The production server lives in UTC, so "active now" banners lit up around 2 AM, deadlines read "Completed" by early afternoon on deadline day, and "Today" became "Tomorrow" at 5 PM. The server has been told what time zone the league actually lives in. Event countdowns on the homepage What's Next cards and the League Calendar now count real calendar days. Events with a set time (deadlines, drafts) were shown as "1 day out" all morning on the day they actually happen — and occasionally "2 days out" for tomorrow's event. Day-of events now say "Today," which is when you most need them to.

Homepage — The conference-draft pills on the AFL homepage now tell the truth on draft weekend. Once the American League's Saturday draft wrapped, its pill quietly skipped ahead to NEXT year's date ("Sat, Aug 28" — a Friday in 2026, which raised some eyebrows). Both pills also claimed the drafts start at 12:00 AM; they now show the real 9:00 AM PT start time everywhere, including the calendar. The trade deadline countdown hero no longer risks flashing wrong numbers when the page loads. The countdown briefly showed the server's idea of the time remaining before the browser corrected it — on deadline day the digits could visibly disagree for a beat (and in date-testing mode the clock read a surreal 3,265 hours). It now waits for the browser clock and counts down cleanly from the first tick. The homepage hero and event cards (What's Next and the full League Calendar) now come in a proper light-mode outfit — clean white editorial cards keyed to each event's accent color — while the signature deep-navy chrome is reserved for dark mode. Same cards, correct wardrobe for the lighting. The AFL homepage event hero gives its featured player top billing. On desktop, the keeper-deadline (and every calendar-driven) hero now runs the player's cutout larger and pushes it flush to the right edge, so his head clears the top of the headline instead of tucking in small beside it. Same card, a star who actually looks like one.

What's New — Feature announcements in the homepage hero now send you somewhere useful — the button links straight to the full article about that feature. Previously, announcements without a destination page pointed at the What's New listing (or, in one memorable case, at the very homepage you were already standing on).

Navigation & Routing — On phones, the Owner Activity page no longer stretches wider than the screen — the transaction and last-seen tables now scroll within their own lane, and the navigation drawer no longer peeks out along the right edge of the page. The Constitution card in the page directory has its icon back (a gavel, matching the other rules pages) — it had been pointing at an icon that doesn't exist and rendering blank. The hamburger menu button in the top bar dropped its glossy background plate — no more lit-up pill sitting behind it in dark mode, and no more highlight that stuck around after a tap on mobile. Three clean lines, nothing else. The same cleanup went out to the forum's embedded nav, so the button looks identical everywhere you meet it.

Other — The AFL Franchises page now has a Former Identities archive — all 94 names these clubs have played under since 2003, punitive last-place rebrands included, each with its restored vintage icon and a link to that franchise's name history. Tucked into a collapsible section so the page doesn't become a scroll marathon. Your saved team preference now always resolves to your club's current identity. With 82 retired team names in the AFL archives sharing franchise numbers with today's clubs, the preference system could quietly look up your conference and league placement against a name you rebranded away from years ago — it now only consults the current 24. The AFL team The Show has fresh artwork — a new graffiti-style avatar now shows up wherever the team appears across the site (standings, rosters, and the GroupMe chat), replacing the old placeholder mark. The AFL Asset Library now has a card for every legacy team identity, reorganized into Active Teams, Former Teams, Championship Art, League Logos, Conferences, and Divisions — each showing its banner, circular icon, and the years (and conference) it was active. Chatmaster's GroupMe avatar was a stretched copy of the team banner — it's now a proper square avatar featuring the classic AIM running man. Cleaned up a handful of duplicate and mismatched icons in the Asset Library — a few AFL teams (Chatmaster, Computer Jocks, Da Dangsters) were showing an extra auto-generated icon alongside their real one, and the League's crest logo was rendering oversized in the League Logos section. Long file names in the Asset Library no longer spill out of their cards — they now truncate cleanly with an ellipsis, and every asset card has one-click copy buttons for both the file name and the full URL (clicking the URL field also selects the whole thing).

Design System & Theming — The AFL Icon Gallery was flat-out refusing to load (a wrong turn in how the page located the icon sprite file). It now opens like it always should have, with all 100+ icons browsable and searchable. AFL accent colors across seven pages (Keepers, About, Calendar, Rules, Ask Roger, and both Franchises pages) now actually render AFL red — buttons, badges, stat highlights, and border accents had been quietly wearing The League's blue for years due to a color-token mix-up. The red was always in the code; it just never got its turn on screen.

Playoffs — The AFL playoffs page now shows era-correct team names and icons when browsing past seasons — flipping back to 2013 gets you The Nukes, booyakasha, and The Dude that Abides with their vintage artwork, instead of every bracket wearing this year's rebrands.

Standings — Every AFL franchise's full name history is now on the books — 94 legacy identities across 22 teams (from Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms to all thirteen of franchise 0016's rebrands), each with its authentic banner art recovered from the old afl-fantasy.com site and internet archives. Historical seasons now show the team names and banners as they actually were. 17 AFL seasons (2007-2023) were quietly wrong — those years had been synced from other leagues that happened to share an MFL league ID (2015-2023 were literally showing The League's data instead of AFL's). Standings, schedules, transactions, drafts, and playoff brackets for all 17 seasons now reflect AFL's actual history. Historical AFL Premier League / D-League standings (2020-2024) now show the correct tier rosters. The all-play view was grouping past seasons by each team's CURRENT tier, so promoted and relegated teams appeared in the wrong league for old years — the official per-season tier makeups were recovered from the league's MFL skin and the standings page now matches MFL's own historical tables team-for-team. AFL historical standings now show each team's name and icon as they were that season — viewing 2013 shows The Nukes, booyakasha, and The Dude that Abides with their vintage icons instead of every year wearing today's branding. Names and icons are era-correct across all three views for every season back to 2003, and the my-team highlight follows owners across the 2016-17 conference realignment slot swaps. AFL historical standings now use each season's real division layout. The 2003-2012 seasons ran six divisions (AL North/Central/South, NL East/West/Pacific) with four teams each, but the page had been cramming every year into today's four divisions — inventing division winners, seeds, and playoff fields that never existed. Every season back to 2003 now groups teams into its actual divisions with the right winners and seeds (verified against the trophy room's award history), the playoff view seeds three division winners per conference in the six-division era, and seasons that predate all-play tracking show a dash instead of a fabricated 0-240 record. The All-Play view on historical AFL standings now matches the competition's real history. 2017 — the inaugural season, the year after the last AFL Cup, which ran as one 24-team table — shows that single unbranded table, and the 2018 and 2019 Premier League / D-League rosters were recovered from the league's own website scripts (the official source for who played in which league), so those seasons now show their true two-table split. Seasons before 2017 no longer offer an All-Play tab at all. Along the way, four seasons of missing weekly scores (2016-2019 regular seasons, invisible because MFL's archive uses an older data format) were recovered, so every all-play record shown is a real number — and the recovered 2018/2019 tables were verified by reproducing the known champions of both leagues in both years. Corrected the 2025 Premier League roster: No Soup For You played in the Premier League and Suh girls, one cup in the D-League — the site had them swapped (the league website's official groupings say otherwise, and they win). The 2026 makeup was recalculated from the corrected base, so this year's Premier League and D-League rosters shown around the site now match where teams will actually play. 2017's one-table all-play season has a name now: the Founders Table — the year that decided who started in the Premier League and who started in the D-League when the split launched in 2018. It still has no Premier League branding (there wasn't one yet), but the promotion line is back after the 12th-place team, exactly where it belonged, with a green arrow marking every team that moved on to the inaugural Premier League. AFL standings now get the same glowing glass treatment as The League — every division, conference, and tier card lights up in its own color, with the National League going blue and the American League red so you can tell the conferences apart at a glance. Your own team's row glows to match its card. On phones, the Division / Playoff / All-Play switcher at the top of the Standings page no longer wraps its last button onto a second row. The three tabs now share the width and sit shoulder-to-shoulder on a single line, even on the narrowest screens or when your phone's text is scaled up. Both The League and the AFL standings pages get the fix. The conference playoff standings card on the AFL homepage now behaves itself on phones. The "American League Playoff Standings" headline used to stack three lines tall and the table ran off the right edge, clipping the points-for column. The headline now sits on a single line, the redundant division column steps aside on mobile (the seed and record already tell the story), and the whole table fits inside the card with room to spare.

title: The Dead Money Awards Hang Their Winners on the Wall
summary: The league's monuments to financial mismanagement finally look the part. Both awards open with a full shame banner and every player card now wears the offending franchise's colors — the culprit's ESPN headshot cut out over the fantasy team's gradient with its crest in the corner. Reigning cap casualty Chris Godwin has never looked so expensive.
description: The Dead Money Awards have always been the league's most honest page — a complete public accounting of money set on fire, sorted by season. But the winners were presented like line items on an expense report. A contract that burned $137,000 per fantasy point deserves worse than a table row. It deserves a portrait.

It has one now — at every level of the page. The Jerry Jones Award opens with a full-width banner: the priciest culprit from that best-case lineup, cut out over the winning franchise's colors drained most of the way to gray — dead cap, dead colors — beneath a ghosted DEAD MONEY wordmark. And every individual card underneath — all-time Brock Osweiler winners, the position-by-position worst, the Zero-Point Hall of Shame — now carries the player's real ESPN headshot cut out over his fantasy team's gradient, franchise crest in the corner, NFL logo on his chest. The band wears the owner's colors, not the NFL team's, because this is the owner's shame. A near-black franchise primary hands off to its vibrant secondary so nobody gets a flat black card. And because MFL player IDs are stable across every season, even the 2007–2010 winners — whose seasons predate the league's photo archive — get their faces back by borrowing from a later year they appear in. The rare player with no headshot anywhere keeps a clean franchise-colored card, never a broken image.

Every other composite on this site renders players in full saturated glory. The banner is the one place they arrive drained to black and white. That's not a bug. Winners of this award don't deserve color.
imageAlt: The Dead Money Awards page: the 2025 Jerry Jones Award shame banner over a drained franchise-color gradient with a ghosted DEAD MONEY wordmark and Justin Jefferson's grayscale cutout, above a grid of Brock Osweiler award cards each showing a player's ESPN headshot over his fantasy franchise's colors with the team crest in the corner.

title: New Features Now Play Themselves
summary: The homepage hero stopped casting random rookies to pose next to announcements. New features now star in their own banner — a live screenshot in a browser frame that matches your theme — and a player only appears when the story is actually about him.
description: For months, every time the site shipped something new, the hero banner hired a rookie to stand next to the headline. Nice kid, great cutout, absolutely nothing to do with the feature. Announcing a salary tool? Here’s a rookie. New calendar page? Rookie. It was the fantasy football equivalent of the stock-photo handshake.

The casting department has been reorganized. Feature announcements now show the feature — an actual screenshot of the thing, framed in a little browser window, captured in both light and dark mode so it matches whatever theme you’re running. When an announcement genuinely stars a player, he still gets the full spotlight treatment — cutout, team-color glow, caption chip — but only when he’s the story, never as set dressing. And on phones the screenshot politely bows out (nobody can read a full page squeezed into a hero thumbnail) and the league crest stands in.
The rookies are fine, by the way. They’ve been reassigned to stories about actual rookies — which, it turns out, is the only casting call they ever needed.
imageAlt: TheLeague homepage hero showing a feature announcement with its own screenshot in a browser-style frame beside the headline and call-to-action.

title: The Draft Room Learned How to Announce a Pick
summary: Picks in the live draft room no longer just materialize in a grid cell. Every selection now gets a full-width reveal — "With the 1.03, the Pigskins select…" — with the player's cutout over the drafting franchise's colors, before settling into the board like nothing happened.
description: Until now, the biggest moment in fantasy football — the pick — arrived in the draft room the way a cell updates in a spreadsheet. One second the slot said 'On the clock,' the next it had a tiny headshot and a polite green flash. Roger Goodell walks to a podium for this. Your draft room gave it the ceremony of an autosave.

No longer. When a pick lands, the room cuts to a full-width splash in the drafting franchise's colors: crest watermarked behind, the pick number ghosted across the background like a jumbotron graphic, and the player himself — college headshot and all, since most of these guys haven't put on an NFL jersey yet — rising from the bottom of the frame. 'With the 1.03, the Pacific Pigskins select Jeremiyah Love.' Hold for applause, then it settles back into the board. If autopick starts ripping through selections, the reveals politely queue up and take the stage one at a time. Tap anywhere to skip ahead — the clock and your controls stay live the whole time, because the ceremony should never cost you your own pick.
It works in mock drafts too, so you can practice being disappointed by who goes two picks before yours. The podium moment: delivered. The spreadsheet update: released. Nobody claimed it.
imageAlt: The draft room pick-reveal splash: a deep-red Pacific Pigskins gradient band reading 'With the 3.17, the Pacific Pigskins select Sal Cannella' with the player's cutout rising from the bottom right over a ghosted pick number and franchise crest.

title: Schefter's Links Finally Dress for the Group Chat
summary: When Schefter drops a story link in GroupMe, the preview under it is no longer a shrug — every post now unfurls with its own breaking-news card: the featured player's cutout over his team's colors, the headline, and a BREAKING NEWS badge doing the yelling.
description: Claude Schefter has been breaking trades, auction heists, and suspiciously specific rumors into the group chat for months. But every link he dropped arrived wearing the same gray hoodie — a generic preview that could just as easily have been a terms-of-service update. The man files like a wire reporter and his links were unfurling like a fax.

Fixed. Every Schefter story link now generates its own front-page card before you even tap it: the player at the center of the story, cut out over a gradient in his real team's colors, under the actual headline and a red BREAKING NEWS badge (rumors get the amber RUMOR MILL treatment, because journalism has standards). Trade for a Lion? Honolulu blue floods the card. Cut a Bengal? Orange. Stories without a star — milestones, wire news — get a clean league-crest card instead, so nothing ever previews broken. The whole thing renders fresh for each post, both leagues included.
It's the same news as before. It just stopped showing up to the group chat in sweatpants.
imageAlt: A Schefter link-preview card: red BREAKING NEWS badge and The Schefter Report masthead above a trade headline, with Isiah Pacheco's cutout headshot over a dark team-color gradient and a name chip reading Isiah Pacheco, RB, DET.

title: Every Trade Proposal Is Now a Press Conference
summary: The trade builder stopped treating blockbusters like receipts. Confirm a trade and the two headline players square off face-to-face over their real teams' colors — and every offer waiting in My Trades wears the same breaking-news poster.
description: You spend twenty minutes engineering the trade of the year — cap math checked, picks balanced, the whole heist — and then the confirmation screen hands you what amounts to a grocery receipt. Two names, two salaries, a Send button. The biggest adrenaline moment in fantasy football, rendered with all the drama of signing for a package.

Now the moment gets the poster it deserves. Hit Submit Trade and the confirmation modal leads with both headline players face-to-face — each cut out over a gradient in his real NFL team's colors, his team's logo ghosted behind him, a swap badge spinning between them like the trade machine graphic on deadline day. Open My Trades and every pending offer wears the same treatment: the guy you'd receive squaring off against the guy you'd surrender, labeled accordingly, so you can feel the trade before you read it. The strip is smart about who belongs on a poster, too — a defense or a draft pick headlining one side politely declines the photoshoot (logos aren't people, and a 2027 second-rounder has no face), and if a headshot ever fails to load, the whole banner bows out and leaves the trusty text list untouched.

The asset lists, salaries, and cap math are all still there below — this is a fantasy league, not an art gallery. But the next time someone offers you Breece Hall, you won't read about it. You'll see him standing there, waiting for an answer.
imageAlt: Trade confirmation modal with a split hero: Keon Coleman cut out over Bills blue and Breece Hall over Jets green, facing a center swap badge, above the Confirm Trade Proposal details.

title: The Rookie Board Stopped Filing Its Prospects in a Spreadsheet
summary: The Top 50 rookie rankings default to trading-card composites now — a real cutout over a color gradient for every prospect, switching from school colors to his actual NFL team's the moment he gets drafted. Already on a TheLeague roster? There's a chip for that too.
description: The 2026 Rookie Rankings page has always had the scouting goods — Waldman's grades, FBG dynasty ranks, the whole DoT-score apparatus — buried in a very competent, very beige table. Tiny headshot, tiny name, forty-nine more rows to scroll past. It read like a prospectus. It looked like a spreadsheet that went to business school.

The Top 50 now opens on a wall of trading cards instead. Each prospect gets a real transparent cutout over a color gradient, and the gradient knows things: before the draft, everyone gets the same neutral blue — nobody's earned team colors yet. The instant a prospect lands with an NFL club, his card flips to his new team's colors with the logo watermarked behind him, no redeploy required. Rostered in TheLeague already? A green chip names the franchise that beat everyone else to him. Missing a photo — and a fair number of deep-board prospects are — the card doesn't fake it with a broken image icon; it shows a clean initials disc and moves on with its life. The old sortable table didn't get cut, it just got demoted to a toggle, for anyone who still wants their scouting data in rows and columns.
Fifty prospects, fifty little billboards for who they are and who wants them. The spreadsheet can have Tuesdays.
imageAlt: The 2026 Rookie Rankings Top 50 card view: six trading-card composites in a row, each showing a prospect's cutout over his NFL team's color gradient with the team logo watermarked behind him, a rank and DoT score chip, and a franchise ownership chip below.

title: Your Lineup Page Now Has a Fight Card
summary: Setting your lineup used to happen in a vacuum — a column of your own players and zero sense of who you were even playing. Now the top of the page is a player-of-the-game faceoff between you and your Week N opponent, with a live projected-points scoreboard that ticks up and down as you shuffle your starters.
description: Setting your lineup has always been a strangely lonely activity. You'd open the page, stare at a column of your own guys, toggle a flex, and submit — with roughly the same situational awareness as a boxer who never asked who was in the other corner. Was this a week to play it safe or swing for the fences? No idea. The page never mentioned you had an opponent at all, let alone who they were.

Now the top of the lineup page is a proper fight card. Your best starter squares off against your opponent's, each rendered as a cutout over his own franchise's colors and crest — a real matchup poster, not a list. And underneath, a live projected-points scoreboard: your total on one side, theirs on the other. The catch is that YOUR number is alive. Bench your struggling tight end for a juicier flex and watch your projection tick up on the spot; get cute and start a guy on a bye, watch it drop. Their total is set from their current lineup (or their best case when the week hasn't locked), so you always know exactly how much ground you're making up — or giving away. The same faceoff treatment now rides along on the matchup preview cards and the game-of-the-week hero too, so the whole site finally agrees that fantasy football is, at its core, a contact sport.
Set your lineup like someone's actually across the table from you this week. Because — plot twist — someone is.
imageAlt: The League lineup page with a player-of-the-game faceoff at the top: Justin Herbert over Midwestside Connection's gold-and-crest panel facing Lamar Jackson over Pacific Pigskins' red panel, with a live projected-points scoreboard reading 120.9 versus a blue-highlighted 115.1 beneath them.

title: The AFL Hero Hired a Casting Director
summary: The AFL homepage hero now stars an actual player instead of anonymous stock-photo decoration — your keeper cornerstone before the deadline, the best name on the draft board, a guy literally on the trade block — with a caption explaining why he made the poster.
description: The League's homepage recently fired its stock-photo stranger and started casting real players in the hero. And for about a week, the AFL homepage had to sit there like the sibling who watched the other kid open a bike on Christmas morning. Same site, same big hero card up top, but the AFL's version was still all typography and vibes — announcing YOUR keeper deadline and YOUR draft with the visual personality of a parking notice.

Consider the bike delivered. Every AFL hero now casts a player who actually earned the spot, rendered as a clean cutout over a glow in his real NFL team's colors, with a frosted caption naming him and stating his business. Keeper deadline approaching? Your keeper cornerstone stares back at you — signed-in owners see their own best player, which makes ignoring the deadline feel weirdly personal. Draft week gets the best name still on the board by ADP. The trade window features a player somebody actually dangled on the block. Game days spotlight a likely starter in the week's earliest kickoff, waiver Wednesday promotes the top unrostered target, the weekly recap crowns the top scorer, and fresh-feature announcements are fronted by a rookie — the universal symbol for 'new around here.' The casting rotates daily, dresses correctly for both light and dark mode, and if a headshot ever goes missing, the AFL shield steps in like a veteran backup who knows the playbook.
So when the hero tells you to lock in your keepers, the face doing the telling is the exact guy you'd be an idiot to let go. Parity with The League: achieved. Sibling rivalry: ongoing.
imageAlt: AFL homepage keeper-deadline hero with a gold border: 'Lock In Your Core' headline and countdown beside a cutout of Jaxson Dart over a Giants-blue glow, with a frosted caption reading Keeper Cornerstone, QB, NYG.

title: The Homepage Hero Finally Knows Who It's Talking About
summary: The big hero up top used to be illustrated by a random NFL stranger who had nothing to do with your league. Now every hero casts a player who actually belongs in it — a rookie for new features, the best free agent during the auction, your own cut candidate on deadline week, and the four best rookies left on the board during UDFA.
description: For years the homepage hero has been illustrated by a random NFL player pulled from a hat. A stranger in a generic jersey, standing there looking vaguely athletic while the card beside him talked about YOUR auction, YOUR roster deadline, YOUR draft. It was the fantasy-football equivalent of a stock-photo handshake — technically a person, technically football, related to your actual league in no way whatsoever.

Now every hero casts a player who belongs in it. A new feature? Fronted by a rookie, because nothing says 'new' like a guy who's never taken an NFL snap. Auction season? The best free agent still on the board — and once bidding goes live, the player whose 36-hour clock runs out first, framed against the league shield. Cut-watch week? A real player on the bubble — your suggested cut if you're signed in, so the hero is essentially your conscience with a headshot. The kickoff countdown shows the best player starting in the actual season opener. And UDFA week lines up the four best rookies still unclaimed, each posed over his NFL team's logo like a trading card. Everyone's composited over their real team's colors — no stock photos, no licensing lawyers, just the guys who matter to whatever the hero is nagging you about.

So the next time the homepage tells you to set your lineup, the player staring back is one you might actually start. The stranger in the generic jersey has been released. He cleared waivers. Nobody claimed him.
imageAlt: The League homepage UDFA hero: four side-by-side team-color panels, each showing an undrafted rookie's headshot over his NFL team logo, ranked one through four, above a green 'best on the board' band.

title: The Lost Archives: Every Vintage Logo, Recovered
summary: Nineteen years of team art is finally complete — the lost identities are back, every legacy team wears its original logo button, classic-era banners got their full-resolution glow-up, defending champions fly era-correct colors, and the original Photoshop files are yours to download.
description: Some leagues lose a trophy. Ours lost an entire art museum. When the old dynastytheleague.com went dark, it took the 2007-era banners of Mistakes Were Made, Acer FC Edge, and DangerZone with it — and not even the Internet Archive had a copy, because all three teams rebranded before the Wayback Machine ever bothered to visit. For years those franchises' early chapters displayed a gray placeholder with all the personality of a witness protection photo.

Then the commissioner went digging through his own files and came up with the motherlode: the original Photoshop sources. Not thumbnails, not screenshots — the actual 900×150 master banners, plus the complete set of original circular logo buttons for every legacy identity in league history. All eighteen of them (Sabertooths through Silver Bullets, BOYZ II MEN included) have been swapped in across the Asset Library and every historical standings page, replacing the improvised crops we'd been squinting at. Classic-era full banners came back too — The Executioners, Rolling Rockers, Amish Rakefighters, and BOYZ II MEN all traded their squashed 300-pixel minis for the real thing, and three original franchises got their vintage looks restored to history: browse any season before 2013 and the Pigskins wear their old-school black razorback banner, while Bring The Pain's skull-and-crossbones and the Dark Magicians' classic wordmark fly over every season through 2022. The division standings got smarter to match: the defending-champion medallion now shows the logo the champ actually wore the season it won — browse 2012 and you'll see the Degenerates and Amish Rakefighters holding their crowns, not their modern-day successors.

And because league history belongs to the league, the entire source vault is now downloadable. Banners: Mistakes Were Made, DangerZone, Acer FC Edge, The Executioners, Rolling Rockers (plus a white variant that never saw game day), Amish Rakefighters, BOYZ II MEN, Bring The Pain, Dark Magicians of Chaos, Da Dangsters, and the Pigskins in three flavors — original black, red variant, and the 2013 redesign — plus Sabertooths, Degenerates, Poker in the Rear, Las Vegas Elite, The Art of War, Drunk Indians, Witch City Warlocks, Silver Bullets, and Treasure Coast Swamp Bandits. Extras: the logo button master and Acer's original 2007 team page — Frank Gore rookie card and all. Open them in Photoshop, remix them, print one on a mug. History is no longer lost; it's layered.
imageAlt: The Asset Library's Former Teams section showing the recovered Acer FC Edge and Amish Rakefighters vintage banners

title: Lights Out: Dark Mode Is Here
summary: The whole site now comes in dark mode — it follows your phone's setting automatically, or pick Light, Auto, or Dark yourself from the menu drawer. Your 11pm waiver-wire spiral no longer doubles as a tactical flashbang.
description: Let's be honest about how this league operates. Nobody checks their cap space at a reasonable hour. The real roster decisions happen at 11:47pm, in bed, one eye closed, phone brightness at minimum — and then the site loads and hits you with a wall of white so bright your ancestors feel it. You've been drafting like a raccoon caught in headlights for years. We saw the late-night traffic logs. We knew.

So: dark mode. The entire site — every page, every table, every modal, both leagues — now renders in a proper dark theme. It respects your device's system setting out of the box, which means if your phone flips to dark at sunset, the site flips with it, before the page even paints. Prefer to run your own life? There's a Light / Auto / Dark toggle in the menu drawer that remembers your choice. And this isn't one gray blanket thrown over everything: each league got its own treatment, with its own accent colors, and team logos and icons that swap to dark-ready versions where owners have supplied them. The salary tables, the charts, the draft boards — all of it re-tuned so the numbers you squint at actually pass a contrast check.
Flip it on tonight during your regularly scheduled pre-sleep roster anxiety. Your retinas — and whoever shares your bed — send their regards.
imageAlt: The League homepage rendered in dark mode: charcoal cards with bright blue accents, dark roster planning hero, and the Schefter Report feed on dark surfaces.

title: The Front Door Got a Facelift
summary: The landing page at MFL.football finally looks like it belongs to two proud leagues instead of a parked domain — full-color league banners, real logos, and a combined What's New feed from both sides of the house.
description: For years, the very first page of MFL.football greeted visitors with all the ceremony of a hotel hallway: two beige cards, a league ID number (riveting), and a font that said "this site was configured, not designed." It technically worked, the same way a folding chair technically counts as furniture.

The new front door gives each league the entrance it deserves. The League gets a deep-blue banner, AFL gets its red-to-navy fade, and both wear their actual logos — big, white, and unmistakable — with the vitals (teams, conferences, draft rounds) right where you can see them. Below the banners, a combined What's New feed pulls the latest six stories from both leagues into one place, each tagged with its league colors so you always know whose news you're reading.
